title: The Bill of Rights
description: Explain how the Bill of Rights protects individual liberties and rights

assessment_items.question_content:
>“[I]t has already been incontrovertibly shown that on the present occasion a bill of rights was totally unnecessary, and that it might be accompanied with some inconveniency and danger if there was any defect in the attempt to enumerate the privileges of the people. This system proposes a union of thirteen sovereign and independent states in order to give dignity and energy to the transaction of their common concerns. It would be idle, therefore to countenance the idea that any other powers were delegated to the general government than those specified in the Constitution itself.”

>Justice Thomas McKean at the Pennsylvania State Convention to ratify the Constitution, edited by John Back McMaster and Frederick D. Stone, 1788

**Which of the following best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A Bill of Rights would potentially give the federal government more power than is explicitly stated in the Constitution
It is necessary to add a Bill of Rights because the Constitution gives the federal government too much power
A Bill of Rights should only protect civil liberties at the state level, not the federal level
A Bill of Rights is unnecessary because it would give too much power to the thirteen states

assessment_items.question_content:
>“[I]t has already been incontrovertibly shown that on the present occasion a bill of rights was totally unnecessary, and that it might be accompanied with some inconveniency and danger if there was any defect in the attempt to enumerate the privileges of the people. This system proposes a union of thirteen sovereign and independent states in order to give dignity and energy to the transaction of their common concerns. It would be idle, therefore to countenance the idea that any other powers were delegated to the general government than those specified in the Constitution itself.”

>Justice Thomas McKean at the Pennsylvania State Convention to ratify the Constitution, edited by John Back McMaster and Frederick D. Stone, 1788

**Which development most directly addressed the concerns expressed by Justice McKean in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The adoption of the Ninth Amendment, which protects rights not listed in the Constitution
The passage of the Fourteenth Amendment, which applied the Bill of Rights to the states
The inclusion of the Supremacy Clause, which gives national law precedence over state law
The creation of the Necessary and Proper Clause, which grants Congress flexibility to carry out its duties

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There are other good things of less moment. I will now add what I do not like [about the Constitution]. First the omission of a bill of rights providing clearly & without the aid of sophisms for freedom of religion, freedom of the press, protection against standing armies, restriction against monopolies, the eternal & unremitting force of the habeas corpus laws, and trials by jury in all matters of fact triable by the laws of the land & not by the law of Nations. . . . Let me add that a bill of rights is what the people are entitled to against every government on earth, general or particular, & what no just government should refuse or rest on inference.” 

>-Thomas Jefferson, [letter to James Madison](https://founders.archives.gov/documents/Madison/01-10-02-0210), 20 December 1787 

**Which of the following statements best summarizes Jefferson’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A bill of rights is necessary to protect civil liberties from infringement from the federal government
A bill of rights is unnecessary because listing some rights will lead to government regulation of those rights
A bill of rights is unnecessary because state constitutions already protect the rights of the people
A bill of rights is necessary to protect civil rights like equal protection under the law

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the Bill of Rights for this Commonwealth it is declared that the happiness of the people & the Preservation of civil government depend upon the piety religion & morality & that the people have a right to invest their Legislature with power to require that provision be made for the public worship of God & the support of protestant teachers & require the attendance of people upon such worship instructions.... We must insist that the Continental Constitution contain a Bill of Rights which by Express shall secure to us our privileges especially our religion.”

> Daniel Adams, at a town meeting in Townshend, Massachusetts, 1787

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A bill of rights would protect civil liberties such as freedom of religion
Each state constitution should adopt a bill of rights specific to that state
Listing specific rights in the Bill of Rights will lead to the regulation of those rights
The Constitution creates a system of checks and balances that protects individual liberties without a bill of rights

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There are other good things of less moment. I will now add what I do not like [about the Constitution]. First the omission of a bill of rights providing clearly & without the aid of sophisms for freedom of religion, freedom of the press, protection against standing armies, restriction against monopolies, the eternal & unremitting force of the habeas corpus laws, and trials by jury in all matters of fact triable by the laws of the land & not by the law of Nations. . . . Let me add that a bill of rights is what the people are entitled to against every government on earth, general or particular, & what no just government should refuse or rest on inference.” 

>-Thomas Jefferson, [letter to James Madison](https://founders.archives.gov/documents/Madison/01-10-02-0210), 20 December 1787 

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Bill of Rights places necessary restrictions on the federal government’s power
The Bill of Rights could potentially be used to limit civil liberties by state governments
The amendment process makes it too easy for citizens to change the structure of the federal government
The United States Constitution successfully places limits on the federal government without adding the Bill of Rights

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the Bill of Rights for this Commonwealth it is declared that the happiness of the people & the Preservation of civil government depend upon the piety religion & morality & that the people have a right to invest their Legislature with power to require that provision be made for the public worship of God & the support of protestant teachers & require the attendance of people upon such worship instructions. . . . We must insist that the Continental Constitution contain a Bill of Rights which by Express shall secure to us our privileges especially our religion.”

> Daniel Adams, at a town meeting in Townshend, Massachusetts, 1787

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Individual liberties must be secured against governmental intrusions by specific language in foundational documents
The Bill of Rights could potentially be used to limit individual liberties
The United States Constitution includes protections for individual liberties and a bill of rights is unnecessary
The structure of the legislative branch ensures that individual liberties are protected

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the Bill of Rights for this Commonwealth it is declared that the happiness of the people & the Preservation of civil government depend upon the piety religion & morality & that the people have a right to invest their Legislature with power to require that provision be made for the public worship of God & the support of protestant teachers & require the attendance of people upon such worship instructions.... We must insist that the Continental Constitution contain a Bill of Rights which by Express shall secure to us our privileges especially our religion.”

> Daniel Adams, at a town meeting in Townshend, Massachusetts, 1787

**Supporters of Adams’s view that a bill of rights is necessary to protect individual liberties could point to which of the following cases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Wisconsin v. Yoder* (1972)
*Baker v. Carr* (1962)
*Brown v. Board of Education, I* (1954)
*Schenck v. United States* (1919)

assessment_items.question_content:
**What statement best explains how civil liberties are protected in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They are guaranteed in the Bill of Rights and defined through Supreme Court rulings.
They are implied by the structure of the Constitution rather than directly stated.
They are secured through laws passed by Congress and signed by the president.
They are mainly covered by state constitutions and local government decisions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines civil liberties?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Freedoms that protect citizens, opinions, and property against government abuse of power
Rights given to the states to protect them from the federal government superseding their power
Rights that guarantee individuals freedom from discrimination at both the state and federal levels of government
Freedoms that give people the right to refuse to obey laws as a peaceful form of political protest

assessment_items.question_content:
**When the Bill of Rights was ratified in 1789, the amendments protected civil liberties infringement from which levels of government? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The federal government only
State governments only
The federal government and state governments
State and local governments

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of civil liberties and civil rights?**

 | Civil liberties | Civil rights
- | - | -
**A** | Right to due process | Freedom from discrimination 
**B** | Freedom of religion  | Freedom of assembly
**C** | Freedom of speech  | Right to petition the government
**D** | Right to vote | Right to privacy

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios represents a violation of one of the civil liberties protected by the Bill of Rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A state compels Amish students to attend school past the eighth grade
A school district segregates its students into schools based on the students’ race
A state creates a racially gerrymandered district to meet requirements in the Voting Rights Act
A law is passed in Congress which bans guns in schools because of the effect it has on interstate commerce

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes a reason why the Framers decided to include a Bill of Rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It was specifically designed to protect individual liberties and rights from a strong central government
It was specifically designed to articulate the enumerated and implied powers of the federal government
It was specifically designed to determine what powers were given to the federal government and what powers were given to the states
It was specifically designed to outline the amendment process and extend voting rights to disenfranchised groups

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would be an example of a violation of one of the civil liberties protected by the Bill of Rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A federal court refuses to provide a lawyer for a defendant who cannot afford one
A state gerrymanders a district giving one political party an advantage when attempting to get candidates elected
A restaurant refuses to serve a group of people because of their race
A state refuses to issue a marriage license to a same-sex couple

assessment_items.question_content:
A city passes a law that bans all protests and public demonstrations within 500 feet of government buildings. A group of citizens that had planned a peaceful protest outside City Hall files a lawsuit, arguing that the law unfairly limits their ability to express their opinions and violates their constitutional rights. Meanwhile, a separate group of residents sues the same city, claiming that a new public housing program is denying Black and Latino applicants at a much higher rate than White applicants. They argue that the city is violating their right to equal protection under the law.

**How do these two cases compare in their constitutional focus?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The first case involves civil liberties, while the second involves civil rights.
The first case involves civil rights, while the second involves civil liberties.
The first case is about political participation, while the second is about criminal justice.
The first case is about criminal justice, while the second is about political participation.
